summary: American soprano (b. 1927), the first African-American prima donna at the Metropolitan Opera. Best known for her portrayals of Aida in Verdi's opera and as Bess in Porgy and Bess (with then-husband William Warfield). Close collaborator with Samuel Barber, premiering his Hermit Songs and the opera Antony and Cleopatra. Made recordings with Herbert von Karajan. Also the first Black singer in a televised opera (Tosca).

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: African-American soprano, the first Black prima donna at the Metropolitan Opera. Made her Met debut in 1961 as Leonora in Verdi's Il Trovatore. Known for her Verdi roles, especially Aida. First Black singer in a televised opera (as Tosca). Made recordings with Herbert von Karajan of Tosca, Carmen, and Verdi's Requiem. Recorded a 1955 Carnegie Hall debut album (later released as 'Rediscovered') with longtime collaborator David Garvey. Wikipedia

comprehensive_summary:
Leontyne Price (born 1927) is an African-American soprano widely considered the first Black prima donna at the Metropolitan Opera, where she made her debut in 1961 as Leonora in Verdi's Il Trovatore. She is particularly known for her Verdi roles, most especially Aida, which she recorded twice — once in 1962 conducted by Georg Solti and once in 1970 conducted by Erich Leinsdorf. In 1960, she was the first African-American lead at La Scala in the role of Aida. She was also the first Black singer to appear in a televised opera, performing the role of Floria Tosca.

In the 1960s, Price made recordings of Tosca, Carmen, and Verdi's Requiem with conductor Herbert von Karajan, who also guided her early appearances in Austria. Her 1955 Carnegie Hall debut (later released as an album called 'Rediscovered') opened with Handel arias and included Brahms's Zigeunerlieder; her longtime collaborator and accompanist was David Garvey. Her opera stage debut was as Madame Lidoine in the US premiere of Poulenc's Dialogues of the Carmelites.

Price was a particularly close collaborator of the American composer Samuel Barber. She premiered his Hermit Songs (a ten-work song cycle for voice and piano setting anonymous poems by Irish monks) at the Library of Congress in 1953, with Barber himself at the piano. Barber also wrote the song cycle Despite and Still for her. She premiered the title female role (Cleopatra) in Barber's opera Antony and Cleopatra at the new Metropolitan Opera House at Lincoln Center in 1966 — a premiere that was considered a disaster. Price also starred in a touring production of Gershwin's Porgy and Bess with her then-husband William Warfield; this production was chronicled by Truman Capote in The Muses Are Heard. In a master class, Price reportedly said that Serena's aria 'My Man's Gone Now' from Porgy and Bess requires a distinctive sighing quality 'like moaning in church.'

cards.front: Performer: first African-American prima donna at the Metropolitan Opera; made her Met debut in 1961 as Leonora in Verdi's Il Trovatore
Performer: best known for Verdi's Aida; first African-American lead at La Scala (1960); recorded Aida twice (with Solti in 1962 and Leinsdorf in 1970)
Performer: first Black singer in a televised opera (as Floria Tosca); made recordings of Tosca, Carmen, and Verdi's Requiem with Herbert von Karajan
Performer: starred in Porgy and Bess with then-husband William Warfield; that production chronicled in Truman Capote's The Muses Are Heard
Performer: opera stage debut as Madame Lidoine in the US premiere of Poulenc's Dialogues of the Carmelites; 1955 Carnegie Hall debut with pianist David Garvey
Performer: premiered Samuel Barber's Hermit Songs (1953) — a ten-song cycle setting Irish monks' poems — with Barber himself at the piano
Performer: premiered the title female role (Cleopatra) in Barber's Antony and Cleopatra at the new Met (1966) — considered a disastrous premiere; Barber's Despite and Still was also written for her
